claim: Multiple flight instructors who worked with Hani Hanjour — the alleged pilot of American Airlines Flight 77 (which struck the Pentagon) — publicly stated that his piloting and English skills were 'well below standard.' The 9/11 Commission Report notes Hanjour was repeatedly advised to discontinue training. In February 2001, an Arizona flight school alerted the Federal Aviation Administration that Hanjour's skills did not match the commercial pilot certificate he already held. In August 2001, instructors at Freeway Airport in Maryland refused to rent him a Cessna after three test flights.

sources.label: 9/11 Commission, “Final Report of the National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States” (2004) — Hanjour passages, esp. pp. 225–226 and notes
New York Times — Jim Yardley, “A Trainee Noted for Incompetence” (May 4, 2002)
ABC News — “FAA Received Alert About 9/11 Hijacker”
sources.quote: I couldn't believe he had a commercial license of any kind with the skills that he had

sources.label: Newsweek — “9/11 Pilot Hani Hanjour Took Extra Flights to Check Out D.C. Airspace”
Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: The flight-instructor concerns are documented in the 9/11 Commission Report and contemporaneous NYT coverage. URLs for the 9/11 Commission Report and the NYT piece are stable but not yet fetched and verbatim-confirmed in this pass. The reel uses this fact (instructor concerns about Hanjour's piloting) to advance the 'remote-controlled planes' conspiracy frame; the underlying instructor reports are documented, but they do not corroborate the remote-control claim.
